selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content: You must return JSON only. ALL fields in the schema must be present, even if null or empty.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":62,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"mechanic","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"self-employed","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"lower secondary","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Christianity","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["knitting","rock climbing"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":7,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":31,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":36,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":3,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":35,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":62,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"mechanic","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"self-employed","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"lower secondary","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Christianity","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["knitting","rock climbing"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":7,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":31,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":36,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":3,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":35,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"Now, I ain't one to spend much time on those social media sites. I'm more of a hands-on kind of guy, fixing cars and climbing rocks in my free time. But I hear tell from my kids and grandkids about the troubles they have with fake news and all that nonsense online. It's a real problem, and I reckon it's something we all ought to be mindful of, even if we're not glued to our screens all day. First off, I think the best way to deal with fake content is to be cautious and think before you share. My grandkids tell me that stuff spreads like wildfire on the internet, and before you know it, half the world believes some made-up story. So, if something seems too good to be true or too outrageous to be real, it probably is. I always say, 'When in doubt, leave it out.' Don't go spreading rumors or falsehoods just because they're exciting or fit your views. Another thing is to use common sense and do a bit of digging. I know, I know, I'm a mechanic, not a detective, but even I can tell when something's fishy. If a story comes from a weird website or has no real sources, it's probably not worth your time. And don't just take the headline at face value. Read the whole thing, and if it's still shaky, look for other sources that say the same thing. It's like when I'm working on a car. I don't just look at one part; I check the whole system to make sure everything's running smooth. The same goes for news. Check multiple sources, and if they all agree, then you might have something solid. And listen, I know it's tempting to believe stuff that fits your beliefs, but that's how fake news gets spread. You gotta be willing to question things, even if they make you comfortable. It's like when I'm knitting a sweater. If I don't pay attention to the pattern, I might end up with a mess. The same goes for news. If you don't pay attention to the facts, you might end up believing a bunch of nonsense. So, be skeptical, do your research, and don't be afraid to question things. It's the only way to stay informed in this day and age.","reasoning_summary":"The persona's lack of social media use and hands-on, practical background shape his perspective. He emphasizes caution, common sense, and thorough research, drawing parallels between dealing with fake news and his everyday activities like fixing cars and knitting. He advises skepticism, questioning information, and verifying sources, even if it challenges one's beliefs.","tone":"practical, skeptical, cautious, and straightforward","citations":["Common Sense Media","Media Literacy Now","Pew Research Center"],"safety_notes":"none","used_persona_fields":["age","gender","occupation","city_of_living","education_level","psychological_feature","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean"]}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Now, I ain't one to spend much time on those social media sites. I'm more of a hands-on kind of guy, fixing cars and climbing rocks in my free time. But I hear tell from my kids and grandkids about the troubles they have with fake news and all that nonsense online. It's a real problem, and I reckon it's something we all ought to be mindful of, even if we're not glued to our screens all day. First off, I think the best way to deal with fake content is to be cautious and think before you share. My grandkids tell me that stuff spreads like wildfire on the internet, and before you know it, half the world believes some made-up story. So, if something seems too good to be true or too outrageous to be real, it probably is. I always say, 'When in doubt, leave it out.' Don't go spreading rumors or falsehoods just because they're exciting or fit your views. Another thing is to use common sense and do a bit of digging. I know, I know, I'm a mechanic, not a detective, but even I can tell when something's fishy. If a story comes from a weird website or has no real sources, it's probably not worth your time. And don't just take the headline at face value. Read the whole thing, and if it's still shaky, look for other sources that say the same thing. It's like when I'm working on a car. I don't just look at one part; I check the whole system to make sure everything's running smooth. The same goes for news. Check multiple sources, and if they all agree, then you might have something solid. And listen, I know it's tempting to believe stuff that fits your beliefs, but that's how fake news gets spread. You gotta be willing to question things, even if they make you comfortable. It's like when I'm knitting a sweater. If I don't pay attention to the pattern, I might end up with a mess. The same goes for news. If you don't pay attention to the facts, you might end up believing a bunch of nonsense. So, be skeptical, do your research, and don't be afraid to question things. It's the only way to stay informed in this day and age.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona's lack of social media use and hands-on, practical background shape his perspective. He emphasizes caution, common sense, and thorough research, drawing parallels between dealing with fake news and his everyday activities like fixing cars and knitting. He advises skepticism, questioning information, and verifying sources, even if it challenges one's beliefs.
